content.text: What CapEx and OpEx Mean in IT

Capital Expenditure (CapEx) and Operational Expenditure (OpEx) are accounting categories that classify how money is spent. In IT, the distinction has profound strategic implications — it determines when costs appear on financial statements, how flexible organisations can be with their infrastructure, and who bears the risk of technology becoming obsolete.

The cloud's shift from CapEx to OpEx is one of the most consequential changes cloud computing brings to enterprise IT. It is a core AZ-900 exam topic and the foundation of the business case for cloud adoption.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of CapEx (on-premises, large upfront costs, 5-year refresh cycles) vs OpEx (cloud, monthly billing matching actual usage)

content.text: Capital Expenditure (CapEx)

CapEx refers to spending on physical assets that the organisation owns and operates. In traditional IT, this means servers, storage arrays, networking hardware, datacenter racks, cooling systems, generators, and the real estate that houses them. These purchases are large, infrequent, and must be made before any value is realised.
How CapEx Works Financially

CapEx spending appears on the balance sheet as an asset and is depreciated over its useful life (typically 3–5 years for IT hardware). This means the cost is spread across multiple accounting periods rather than hitting the income statement all at once:
Year
Event
Balance Sheet Impact
Income Statement (depreciation)
Purchase $500k server cluster
+$500k asset
$0 (not yet depreciated)
Operate servers
$400k remaining asset
-$100k depreciation expense
Continue operations
Declining book value
-$100k/year each year
Hardware fully depreciated
$0 book value
Refresh cycle starts again

The practical problem: you must decide how much to buy before you know demand. Buy too much → idle capacity wasting money for years. Buy too little → performance problems and an emergency procurement process that takes months.
The Hidden Costs of CapEx IT
Facilities
: Power, cooling, physical security, and rack space cost $1,500–4,000 per rack per month in co-location. A 20-rack cluster adds $360,000–$960,000/year before a single server is purchased.
Labour
: Hardware racking, OS installation, patching, storage provisioning, and network configuration require dedicated operations staff.
Redundancy overhead
: High-availability configurations require spare capacity — typically 2× or 3× the minimum needed — so the utilisation rate of a well-designed CapEx environment is often 30–40%.
Insurance and disaster recovery
: Physical assets require insurance. DR requires a secondary site with duplicate hardware.
Opportunity cost
: Capital tied up in depreciating hardware cannot be invested in revenue-generating projects.
Operational Expenditure (OpEx)

OpEx refers to day-to-day operating costs — spending that is fully expensed in the period it occurs. Cloud computing is fundamentally an OpEx model: you pay for the compute, storage, and networking you consume each month, with no upfront purchase and no physical asset ownership.
How OpEx Works Financially
Cloud spending hits the income statement directly in the month it is incurred. There is no capitalised asset to depreciate and no balance sheet impact. This has several financial advantages:
Immediate deductibility
: OpEx is tax-deductible in the period incurred. CapEx deductions are spread across 3–5 years via depreciation, delaying the tax benefit.
No activation energy
: A new project on Azure requires no capital approval. A team with a valid subscription can provision resources in minutes and see the first bill one month later.
Usage-aligned spend
: If demand drops, the cloud bill drops. On-premises infrastructure costs the same whether utilised at 5% or 95%.
Predictable unit economics
: Azure publishes per-hour pricing for every SKU, making cost modelling straightforward before deployment.
Azure Billing: OpEx in Practice

# Estimate cost before deploying (no purchase required — just a calculation)
az pricing list-skus   --resource-type virtualMachines   --location eastus   --query "[?name=='Standard_D4s_v5'].{Name:name}" -o table

# View current month's actual spend by resource group
az consumption usage list   --start-date $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01)   --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d)   --query 'sort_by([].{ResourceGroup:resourceGroup, Service:meterCategory, Cost:pretaxCost}, &Cost)'   --output table

# Set a monthly budget alert (OpEx governance)
az consumption budget create   --budget-name "dev-monthly"   --amount 5000   --time-grain Monthly   --time-period-start 2025-01-01   --time-period-end 2026-01-01   --resource-group dev-rg   --notifications '[{
    "enabled": true, "operator": "GreaterThan",
    "threshold": 80, "contactEmails": ["team@company.com"],
    "thresholdType": "Actual"
  }]'
Comparing CapEx and OpEx for IT
Dimension
CapEx (On-Premises)
OpEx (Cloud)
Payment timing
Large upfront, years before value
Monthly, after consumption
Balance sheet
Capitalised as asset, depreciated over 3–5 years
Fully expensed in the period incurred
Demand flexibility
Provision for projected peak; idle capacity is wasted
Elastic: scale to actual demand, scale down immediately
Technology refresh
Locked into hardware for 3–5 year cycles
Microsoft refreshes platform hardware automatically
Approval process
Capital budget approval, often annual or quarterly
Operational budget; new services provisioned immediately
Risk profile
Risk of over-buy or under-buy; hardware failure risk
Risk of cost drift; vendor dependency; egress costs
Ownership
Organisation owns the physical asset
Microsoft owns infrastructure; you own data and workloads
The Consumption Model: Cloud's Core Pricing Principle

Azure's default pricing is the consumption model — you pay per second, per hour, or per operation for what you use. The pricing calculator at azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator lets you estimate monthly costs for any combination of services before deploying a single resource:

# Key Azure pricing units by service category

# Compute (VMs) — billed per second while running
# Standard_D4s_v5 (4 vCPU, 16 GB) in East US: ~$0.192/hour pay-as-you-go
# Stop (deallocate) the VM and billing stops immediately:
az vm deallocate --resource-group myapp-rg --name myapp-vm
# Restart to resume billing:
az vm start --resource-group myapp-rg --name myapp-vm

# Storage — billed per GB per month (data at rest)
# LRS Blob Storage: ~$0.018/GB/month in East US
# Example: 10 TB = 10,240 GB × $0.018 = ~$184/month

# Networking — billed per GB for outbound (egress)
# First 5 GB/month: FREE; $0.087/GB thereafter (Zone 1)
# Inbound (ingress) to Azure: FREE

# Serverless — billed per invocation and per GB-second
# Azure Functions first 1,000,000 executions/month: FREE
# Additional: $0.20 per million executions
# Execution time: $0.000016 per GB-second (after 400,000 GB-s free tier)
Reserved Instances: CapEx Commitment Within OpEx Cloud

Reserved Instances (RIs) and Azure Savings Plans introduce an element of upfront commitment into the cloud model, blurring the pure CapEx/OpEx distinction. You commit to one or three years of usage in exchange for discounts of up to 72% compared to pay-as-you-go rates:
1-year Reserved Instance
: ~40% discount vs pay-as-you-go. Payment can be monthly or fully upfront (additional discount for upfront).
3-year Reserved Instance
: ~60–72% discount. Suitable for stable, predictable workloads expected to run for the full term.
Azure Savings Plans
: Commit to an hourly spend amount (not a specific VM SKU). More flexible than RIs — applies across any compute service.
Hybrid Benefit
: Bring existing Windows Server or SQL Server licences to Azure, saving up to 40% on top of RI discounts.

# Compare pay-as-you-go vs Reserved Instance cost for a D4s_v5 VM (East US)
# Pay-as-you-go:     $0.192/hour × 8,760 hours/year = $1,682/year
# 1-year RI:         ~$0.112/hour × 8,760 = $981/year  (saving: $701 = 42%)
# 3-year RI:         ~$0.074/hour × 8,760 = $648/year  (saving: $1,034 = 61%)

# Purchase a 1-year RI for 4 D4s_v5 VMs (shared scope — applies to any matching VM)
az reservations reservation-order purchase   --applied-scope-type Shared   --billing-scope /subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID   --display-name "prod-d4sv5-4x-1yr"   --quantity 4   --reserved-resource-type VirtualMachines   --sku Standard_D4s_v5   --term P1Y   --billing-plan Monthly   --location eastus
  # Monthly billing plan: no large upfront payment, still ~40% discount
  # This keeps the commitment as OpEx despite the reservation term

Key insight: Reserved Instances let you capture most of the cost savings of long-term commitment while still keeping charges as OpEx (monthly billing) rather than CapEx (large upfront purchase). Finance teams often prefer this because it avoids balance sheet impact while reducing cloud spend significantly.
TCO Analysis: Quantifying the CapEx-to-OpEx Shift
The Azure Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Calculator helps quantify the full cost of on-premises infrastructure versus equivalent Azure services over a multi-year period. A typical analysis compares:
Server hardware purchase price, amortised over 5 years
Software licensing (OS, middleware, databases)
Datacenter facilities: power, cooling, rack space ($1,500–4,000/rack/month)
IT operations staff time for hardware management, patching, and provisioning
Networking hardware, switches, and WAN connectivity
Backup and disaster recovery infrastructure

Industry analyses consistently show 30–60% total cost reduction over 5 years when migrating to Azure, driven primarily by: higher utilisation (cloud: 60–70% vs on-premises: 15–20%), elimination of facility costs, reduced operations labour, and hardware refresh elimination.

# Azure TCO Calculator is a web tool at:
#   https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/tco/calculator/
# It walks through: servers, databases, storage, networking
# and produces a 5-year comparison report

# For programmatic cost analysis, use Azure Cost Management REST API:
# Get spending breakdown by service for the current month
az rest   --method GET   --uri "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID/providers/Microsoft.Consumption/usageDetails?api-version=2023-03-01&\$filter=properties/usageStart ge '2025-01-01' and properties/usageEnd le '2025-01-31'"   --query 'value[].{Service:properties.meterCategory, Cost:properties.pretaxCost}'   | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
from collections import defaultdict
totals = defaultdict(float)
for row in data:
    totals[row['Service']] += float(row.get('Cost', 0) or 0)
for svc, cost in sorted(totals.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:10]:
    print(f'{cost:>10.2f}  {svc}')
  "
Practical Decision Framework: When to Use Each Model
The CapEx vs OpEx decision is not binary — most mature organisations use a hybrid model. The key is to match the cost structure to the workload characteristics:
Workload Type
Recommended Model
Reasoning
Stable baseline (24/7 web app)
OpEx + Reserved Instance
Predictable demand → 3-yr RI gives ~60% discount vs pay-as-you-go
Variable / spiky demand
OpEx pay-as-you-go + autoscale
Match cost to actual demand; don't over-provision for peaks
Dev/test environments
OpEx pay-as-you-go
Turn off nights/weekends; only pay when team is working
Regulatory data that cannot leave premises
CapEx on-premises + Azure Arc management
Data sovereignty may require owned infrastructure in specific facilities
New greenfield product
OpEx serverless / PaaS
No upfront cost; scale from zero; convert to RI only after demand is proven
AZ-900 Exam Patterns
"A company wants to avoid large upfront IT costs" →
OpEx cloud model (Azure pay-as-you-go).
"An organisation must plan IT spend precisely for 3 years" →
Reserved Instances within OpEx — fixed monthly commitment.
"Which model aligns IT spending with actual resource usage?" →
OpEx / consumption model.
"CapEx vs OpEx: which appears on the balance sheet?" →
CapEx (as a depreciating asset). OpEx hits the income statement directly.
The exam distinguishes CapEx (upfront, ownership, depreciation) from OpEx (ongoing, consumption, expensed immediately). Know both definitions and their cloud implications.
FinOps: Managing OpEx Cloud Spend as a Team Discipline

The shift to OpEx creates a new organisational challenge: cloud costs are now the responsibility of every engineer who provisions a resource, not just a central IT purchasing team. FinOps (Financial Operations) is the practice that addresses this — a collaboration between finance, engineering, and leadership to ensure cloud spending is optimised and accountable.
The FinOps Framework
Inform
: Make cost data visible to the teams that generate it. Azure Cost Management dashboards, cost allocation tags, and chargeback reports let each team see what they are spending.
Optimise

: Identify and act on cost reduction opportunities — right-sizing VMs, deleting idle resources, purchasing Reserved Instances for stable workloads, and using spot/preemptible instances for fault-tolerant batch jobs.
Operate
: Embed cost governance into normal engineering workflows. Every pull request that adds cloud resources should include an estimated monthly cost. Budgets alert before overruns occur.
Azure Cost Management: Implementing FinOps Practices
Identifying and Eliminating OpEx Waste
OpEx flexibility introduces a new risk: costs accumulate silently if unused resources are not cleaned up. Common waste patterns and remediation:
Waste Pattern
Detection Method
Remediation
VMs running 24/7 with < 5% avg CPU
Azure Advisor Recommendations → Cost
Right-size or schedule auto-shutdown
Unattached managed disks
Resource Graph: diskState == Unattached
Snapshot and delete; ~$0.05/GB/month × years adds up
Idle App Service Plans
Azure Monitor: zero requests for 30+ days
Delete or downgrade to Free tier
Unused public IP addresses
Resource Graph: associated == null
Delete; static IPs billed even when unassigned
Dev environments running nights/weekends
Cost Management: spending outside business hours
Azure Automation start/stop schedule or DevTest Labs policies

# Azure Resource Graph: find unattached managed disks across all subscriptions
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type == 'microsoft.compute/disks'
| where properties.diskState == 'Unattached'
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId,
    diskSizeGB=properties.diskSizeGB,
    skuName=sku.name
| order by diskSizeGB desc
" --output table

# Find VMs with < 5% avg CPU (candidates for right-sizing or deletion)
# Run this in Log Analytics:
# Perf
# | where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
# | where ObjectName == 'Processor' and CounterName == '% Processor Time'
# | where InstanceName == '_Total'
# | summarize AvgCPU=avg(CounterValue) by Computer
# | where AvgCPU < 5
# | order by AvgCPU asc
Azure Dev/Test Pricing: OpEx Optimisation for Non-Production

Azure offers significantly reduced rates for development and testing workloads through the Dev/Test subscription offer. This is one of the clearest examples of OpEx flexibility that has no CapEx equivalent — you pay different rates for the same hardware SKUs based on workload classification:
Visual Studio subscriber VMs
: Windows Server VMs used by Visual Studio subscribers for dev/test pay only the Linux rate (no Windows licence surcharge) — typically 40–50% cheaper.
Dev/Test subscriptions
: Offer reduced rates on specific services including App Service, Azure SQL, Logic Apps, and others. Requires Enterprise Agreement or MSDN subscription.
Azure DevTest Labs
: Enforces cost policies on dev environments — maximum VMs per user, automatic shutdown schedules, pre-approved VM sizes, and cost tracking per lab.

# Azure DevTest Labs: enforce auto-shutdown and cost limits
# Create a DevTest Lab with auto-shutdown at 7 PM
az lab create   --resource-group devtest-rg   --name myteam-lab   --location eastus

# Set lab policy: auto-shutdown at 19:00 Eastern
az lab policy set   --resource-group devtest-rg   --lab-name myteam-lab   --policy-set-name default   --name LabVmsShutdown   --status Enabled   --threshold '{"status": "Enabled", "taskType": "LabVmsShutdownTask", "dailyRecurrence": {"time": "1900"}, "timeZoneId": "Eastern Standard Time"}'

# Limit: max 5 VMs per user, max 4 vCPUs per VM
az lab policy set   --resource-group devtest-rg   --lab-name myteam-lab   --policy-set-name default   --name MaxVmsAllowedPerUser   --status Enabled   --threshold 5
Spot Instances: Deep OpEx Discounts for Fault-Tolerant Workloads

Azure Spot VMs offer unused capacity at up to 90% discount compared to pay-as-you-go prices. The trade-off: Azure can reclaim Spot VMs with 30 seconds notice when the capacity is needed for regular workloads. This makes Spot ideal for batch processing, rendering, CI/CD pipelines, and any workload that can checkpoint and resume:

# Create a Spot VM (up to 90% cheaper — evicted when Azure needs capacity)
az vm create   --resource-group myapp-rg   --name spot-worker   --image Ubuntu2204   --size Standard_D8s_v5   --priority Spot   --eviction-policy Deallocate   --max-price -1   --admin-username azureuser   --generate-ssh-keys
  # max-price -1 = pay whatever current spot price is (up to pay-as-you-go max)
  # eviction-policy Deallocate = VM is stopped (not deleted) on eviction
  #   → you can restart it when capacity is available again

# For VMSS: mix spot and on-demand to balance cost and availability
az vmss create   --resource-group myapp-rg   --name mixed-vmss   --image Ubuntu2204   --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5   --instance-count 4   --priority Regular   --single-placement-group false
# Then add spot instances to the same scale set for batch overflow

Spot VM economics: A Standard_D8s_v5 VM costs ~$0.384/hour pay-as-you-go. The same VM as a Spot instance might cost $0.04–0.12/hour depending on capacity availability. A batch job running 10 hours/day would cost $40/month pay-as-you-go vs $4–12/month on Spot — an 70–90% reduction in compute OpEx.
Cost Governance: Preventing OpEx Overruns

The primary risk of OpEx cloud spending is cost drift — resources provisioned and forgotten, or workloads that scale up unexpectedly and generate large bills. A layered governance approach prevents this:
Cloud Economics in a Hybrid World

Most organisations do not make a clean break from CapEx to pure OpEx cloud. They operate in a hybrid model: existing on-premises CapEx investments coexist with new cloud OpEx spending. Azure provides tools to manage and optimise this hybrid cost landscape.
Azure Hybrid Benefit: Reusing CapEx Licence Investments

If your organisation has already purchased Windows Server or SQL Server licences with Software Assurance under a Microsoft Volume Licensing agreement, Azure Hybrid Benefit lets you bring those licences to Azure VMs — eliminating the Windows/SQL software surcharge that is normally included in VM pricing:

# Enable Azure Hybrid Benefit on a new Windows Server VM
az vm create   --resource-group myapp-rg   --name hybrid-vm   --image Win2022Datacenter   --size Standard_D4s_v5   --license-type Windows_Server   --admin-username azureadmin   --admin-password "$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name myapp-kv --name vm-pass --query value -o tsv)"
  # license-type Windows_Server = apply Hybrid Benefit (use existing SA licence)
  # Without this flag: ~$0.192/hour for compute + ~$0.096/hour Windows licence = $0.288/hour
  # With Hybrid Benefit: ~$0.192/hour (Linux rate) = 33% saving on this VM

# Apply Hybrid Benefit to an existing SQL Server on Azure VM
az vm update   --resource-group myapp-rg   --name sql-vm   --license-type AHUB
  # AHUB = Azure Hybrid Use Benefit
  # SQL Server Enterprise on Standard_E16s_v5:
  #   Without AHUB: ~$6.45/hour; With AHUB: ~$1.85/hour (72% saving)

# Check which VMs in a resource group have Hybrid Benefit applied
az vm list   --resource-group myapp-rg   --query "[].{Name:name, LicenseType:licenseType}"   --output table
CapEx Amortisation and Cloud Migration Timing

When planning a cloud migration, the timing relative to existing CapEx depreciation cycles matters financially. Running unexpired hardware through to end of depreciation before migrating avoids writing off assets early. A common approach:
Map each on-premises server to its remaining depreciation life (from the asset register).
Identify servers at or near end of depreciation — these are migration candidates with no write-off cost.
For servers with significant remaining book value, model whether early migration savings (Azure OpEx savings) outweigh the write-off cost.
Use Azure Migrate Assessment to get a per-server cloud cost estimate and project the break-even point.
Migrate hardware that is end-of-life first; plan migrations for mid-cycle hardware around the next refresh date.

# Azure Migrate: discover and assess on-premises VMs for cloud migration
# After deploying the Azure Migrate appliance on-premises:

# Create a migration project
az migrate project create   --resource-group migrate-rg   --project-name myapp-migration   --location eastus

# List discovered servers and their assessment results
az migrate assessment list   --resource-group migrate-rg   --project-name myapp-migration   --query '[].{Name:name, MonthlyComputeCost:properties.monthlyComputeCost, Readiness:properties.suitability}'   --output table
  # The assessment shows: recommended Azure SKU, monthly cost estimate,
  # and migration readiness (Ready / Ready with Conditions / Not Ready)

Financial teams often use a 'cloud-first for new workloads' policy: all net-new workloads go to Azure (OpEx), while existing on-premises workloads migrate at end of their depreciation cycle. This avoids both asset write-offs and the political challenge of changing budget allocations mid-cycle.
Measuring the Business Value of the OpEx Model
Beyond accounting classification, the OpEx cloud model creates measurable business value that can be quantified for stakeholder presentations:
Metric
On-Premises (CapEx)
Azure (OpEx)
Business Value
Time to provision new environment
8–16 weeks (procurement → delivery → rack → configure)
5–30 minutes (Bicep template deploy)
Faster product development cycles
Average server utilisation
15–20% (over-provisioned for peak)
60–70% (right-sized + autoscale)
3–4× more value per dollar of compute spend
Infrastructure ops labour
High: racking, patching, storage admin
Low: platform manages hardware; team focuses on app
Ops engineers redirected to higher-value work
DR cost
2× hardware (primary + DR site)
Geo-replication at fraction of cost; pay only for DR storage
DR coverage without doubling infrastructure spend
Summary
CapEx
: Large upfront spending on owned assets, depreciated over 3–5 years. On-premises IT is CapEx-heavy. Forces organisations to provision for peak demand years in advance.
OpEx
: Ongoing spending expensed in the period incurred. Cloud computing is an OpEx model — pay per hour/GB/invocation for what you consume. Aligns spending with actual usage.
Cloud advantage

: Eliminates upfront hardware purchases, removes facility costs, enables elastic capacity, and allows financial teams to expense cloud costs immediately rather than capitalising and depreciating assets.
Reserved Instances
: A hybrid model — OpEx billing (monthly) with RI-style commitment discounts (up to 72%). Finance teams benefit from predictable monthly costs without balance sheet impact.
The CapEx-to-OpEx shift is the primary financial justification for cloud migration in the AZ-900 framework.
